---
title: "1.1 Introduction to Algorithms, Programming, and Compilers"
subtitle: "Part 2: Compiling, Keywords, and Syntax Errors"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Compiling and Running Java Programs

### Source file, IDE, compiler

Code can be written in any text editor, including the small text editor built into the Active Code exercises in this e-book.

An <span class="term">Integrated Development Environment</span> (IDE, 集成开发环境) is often used because it provides tools to write, compile, and run code.

In other IDEs, you must name the file the same name as the class name with `.java` as the extension.

All Java code must be defined inside a class in a source file, and the name of the class must match the file name.

## Compiler

### Translate and check before running

Computers do not actually speak Java, so we have to <span class="term">compile</span> Java source files into class files that a computer can understand and run.

A <span class="term">compiler</span> (编译器) checks your code for errors and translates your code to executable code that can be run.

Errors detectable by the compiler need to be fixed <span class="mark">before the program can be run</span>.

## Java Keywords

### Reserved words

<span class="term">Keywords</span> are reserved words that have special meaning in Java.

Keywords such as `public`, `class`, and `void` must be in lowercase.

Class names such as `System` and `String` are capitalized.

## Reorder Task

### `hparsons:: mp-main-method-order-1`

Textbook prompt: Drag or click on the blocks you need to move them from the top section into the yellow area to create the first line for the `main` method.

There are extra blocks that you do not need.

```text
public static
void main(
String[]
args)
public Static
```

## Correct Order

### `hparsons:: mp-main-method-order-1`

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

Reject:

```text
public Static
```

## Reorder Task

### `hparsons:: mp-main-method-order-2`

```text
public
static
void
main(
String[]
args)
Void
Public
```

Correct line:

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

## Reorder Task

### `hparsons:: mp-main-method-order-3`

```text
public
static
void
main
(
String
[]
args
)
()
```

Correct line:

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

## Java Statements

### Semicolon

Lines in a Java program that express a complete action such as printing output must end with a semicolon `;`.

Such a line is called a <span class="term">statement</span> (语句).

You can think of the semicolon in Java like a period in English.

You will not be penalized on the exam if you forget a needed semicolon, but the Java compiler is not so lenient.

## Reorder Task

### `hparsons:: mp-print-order-1`

Textbook prompt: Create a Java statement that will print `"Hi!"`.

```text
System
.
out
.
println
(
"
Hi!
"
)
;
system
```

## Correct Order

### `hparsons:: mp-print-order-1`

```java
System.out.println("Hi!");
```

Reject `system` because Java is case-sensitive.

## Reorder Task

### `hparsons:: mp-print-order-2`

Textbook prompt: Create a Java statement that will print `"Bye!"`.

```text
System
.
out
.
println
(
"
Bye!
"
)
;
Out
```

## Correct Order

### `hparsons:: mp-print-order-2`

```java
System.out.println("Bye!");
```

Reject `Out`; the field name is lowercase `out`.

## Syntax Errors and Debugging

### Compiler-reported errors

<span class="term">Syntax errors</span> (语法错误) are reported by the compiler if Java code is not correctly written.

Examples of syntax errors are:

::: {.tight-list}
- a missing semicolon `;`
- an open curly brace `{` without a close curly brace `}`
- an open quote `"` without a close quote `"`
:::

Informally, a syntax error is called a <span class="term">bug</span>, and the process of removing errors is called <span class="term">debugging</span> (调试).

## First Bug

### Grace Hopper's log

::: {.image-fit}
![Grace Hopper's log showing a real bug, 1947](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-1-introduction-to-algorithms-programming-and-compilers/firstbug.jpg){fig-align="center" width="34%"}
:::

An early computer science pioneer Grace Hopper documented a real bug, a moth that flew into a computer in 1947.

## Mixed-Up Program

### `parsonsprob:: mixed-hi`

The following has all the correct code to print out `"Hi my friend!"` when the code is run, but the code is mixed up.

```java
public class Hello
{
   public static void main(String[] args)
   {
      System.out.println("Hi my friend!");
   } // end of main
} // end of class
```

## Mixed-Up Program

### `parsonsprob:: mixed-hi-paired`

The following has all the correct code to print out `"Hi there!"`, but it also contains extra blocks with errors.

Correct blocks:

```java
public class Hello
{
   public static void main(String[] args)
   {
      System.out.println("Hi there!");
   } // end of main
} // end of class
```

## Extra Blocks to Reject

### `parsonsprob:: mixed-hi-paired`

```java
public Class Hello
{
```

```java
public static void main()
{
```

```java
System.out.println("Hi there!")
```

## Reading Error Messages

### Compile-time error

Click run to compile and run code. If there is a syntax error, look for the error message below the code.

This is called a <span class="term">compile time error</span> because it is an error detected by the compiler.

It will be a lot easier to find the problem if you understand how to read the error message.

## Error Message Shape

### File, line, message, caret

```text
FirstClass.java:5: error: unclosed string literal
       System.out.println("Hi there!);
                          ^
1 error
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `FirstClass.java` is the file.
- `5` is the line where the compiler detected the error.
- `unclosed string literal` is the error message.
- `^` points near where the compiler thinks the problem is.
:::

## Error Message Strategy

### Read before fixing

Error messages are not always 100% accurate about where the error actually is.

Sometimes you need to change something earlier in the program, and sometimes a bit later.

The line number is the best place to start looking.

It is worth getting in the habit of really reading error messages.

## Debugging Task

### `activecode:: sc2error1`

Textbook prompt: Fix the code below.

```java
public class FirstClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println("Hi there!);
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: sc2error1`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
Hi there!
```

The visible compiler message points to an unclosed string literal.

## Debugging Task

### `activecode:: sc2error2`

Textbook prompt: Click run, look for an error message after the code, fix the code, and run it again.

```java
public class SecondClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println("Hi there!";
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: sc2error2`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
Hi there!
```

The method call needs a matching closing parenthesis.

## Debugging Task

### `activecode:: sc2error3`

This program has more than one error.

Read the first compiler message, fix one issue, and compile again.

```java
public class ThirdClass
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        system.out.println("Hi there!")
    }
}
```

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: sc2error3`

Runestone expects this output from `main`:

```text
Hi there!
```

Remember that `System` is capitalized in `System.out.println`, and find the missing semicolon.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why Java source code must be compiled before running
- match a class name to a `.java` file name in a regular IDE
- distinguish lowercase Java keywords from capitalized class names
- build `public static void main(String[] args)`
- build `System.out.println("Hi!");`
- read compiler errors using file, line, message, and caret
- fix one compile-time error at a time
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Runtime errors and debugging challenge →](1-1-part-3-runtime-errors-and-debugging-challenge.html)
